gauge.name: Etowah River at GA 9 near Dawsonville

impacts.statement: Major flooding occurs. Highway 9 goes underwater in low areas in south Dawson County and Nicholson and Old Federal Roads are closed in northwest Forsyth County to residents.

Moderate flood begins in south Dawson county and continues in northwest Forsyth county. Widespread flooding of pasture land and horse stables occur. Old Federal and Nicholson Roads are closed in northwest Forsyth county.
Moderate flooding begins in northwest Forsyth county. Old Federal Road begins to flood and Nicholson Road is closed off to residents. Minor flooding continues in south Dawson county.
Flood Stage is reached and minor flooding begins in south Dawson county. Minor flooding continues on Nicholson Road in northwest Forsyth county. The water will be around one foot deep on the road.
Minor flooding of Nicholson Road in northwest Forsyth county begins.
Bankfull is reached on the river in south Dawson county. Some minor flooding begins in pasture lands of northwest Forsyth county and water approaches low areas of Nicholson Road.

properties.caption: View of the canoe launch on the right bank of the Etowah River, downstream of the Highway 9 bridge. Photo taken April 26, 2016, at approximately 4.8 feet stage height.
Upstream view of the Etowah River from the old GA Highway 9 bridge (bridge replaced in 2018). Photo taken April 26, 2016, at approximately 4.8 feet stage height.

Downstream view of the Etowah River from the old GA Highway 9 bridge (bridge replaced in 2018). Photo taken April 26, 2016, at approximately 4.8 feet stage height. Canoe launch in the distance (right bank).

impactsLowWaters.impact: Minimum flow suggested to paddle the 18.2 mile stretch of the Etowah River from Highway 136 to Kelly Bridge Rd.
Paddled the Dawson Forest section and found a few low-water spots and slower current than usual, but still very runnable despite very low water level.
Canoed from the bridge on US 19 west of Dahlonega to the bridge on GA 136.  Low water level made the 12-mile trip last nine hours.  Especially shallow and difficult around Castleberry Bridge.
Minimum flow required to paddle the 65.4 miles from GA 136 to the Knox Bridge boat ramp on Lake Allatoona.  6.5 day excursion.  Class I.
Good flow for paddling the 6.0 miles from Highway 9 to Castleberry Road (above Dawsonville).  River difficulty: Trained beginner (Class I-II)
Nice level for paddling the 2.56 miles from Highway 52 to Highway 9 (above Dawsonville).  River difficulty: Beginner (Class I)
Minimum flow required to paddle the 27.9 mile upper section of the Etowah River from Hightower Church Road to GA 136 above Dawsonville.  Three day trip.  Class I to II.
Bankfull capacity (the amount of water a river can carry without overflowing its banks) at Dawsonville.

hydronotes.statement: Automated gauge readings are now available, courtesy of the USGS.
River forecasts for this location take into account past precipitation and the precipitation amounts expected approximately 48 hours into the future from the forecast issuance time.

value.label: North American Vertical Datum of 1988
value.description: A fixed reference for elevations determined by geodetic leveling. The datum was derived from a general adjustment of the first-order terrestrial leveling nets of the United States, Canada, and Mexico. In the adjustment, only the height of the primary tidal bench mark, referenced to the International Great Lakes Datum of 1985 (IGLD 85) local mean sea level height value, at Father Point, Rimouski, Quebec, Canada was held fixed, thus providing minimum constraint. NAVD 88 and IGLD 85 are identical. However, NAVD 88 bench mark values are given in Helmert orthometric height units while IGLD 85 values are in dynamic heights. See International Great Lakes Datum of 1985, National Geodetic Vertical Datum of 1929, and geopotential difference. NAVD 88 should not be used as Mean Sea Level.

value.label: Station Datum
value.description: A fixed base elevation at a tide station to which all water level measurements are referred. The datum is unique to each station and is established at a lower elevation than the water is ever expected to reach. It is referenced to the primary bench mark at the station and is held constant regardless of changes to the water level gauge or tide staff. The datum of tabulation is most often at the zero of the first tide staff installed.
